a: Document environnemental degradation from agricultural practices through local NGOs and community organizations
Report illegal water extraction or land seizures to international monitoring organizations
Provide data to international research institutions studying Afghan agricultural sustainability
International agricultural companies: Avoid sourcing from supply chains linked to environnemental destruction in Afghanistan
Support alternative crop programs through corporate social responsibility initiatives
Investir dans drought-resistant seed varieties adapted to Afghan conditions
FAO: Expand agricultural extension services and drought-resistant crop programs
UNODC: Continue monitoring and reporting on opium cultivation patterns and alternative livelihood outcomes
World Bank (in trust): Maintain agricultural data collection through the Afghanistan Reconstruction Trust Fund
Support targeted sanctions relief for agricultural development programs that demonstrably reduce environnemental degradation
Fund satellite-based environnemental monitoring of Afghan agricultural land through international climate finance
Advocate for inclusion of Afghan environnemental data in global food security monitoring systems
Support Afghan diaspora organizations working on sustainable agriculture knowledge transfer
i: Note: Under Taliban governance (since August 2021), formal institutional channels are severely constrained. International NGOs operating in Afghanistan include WFP (World Food Programme), FAO (Food and Agriculture Organization), and ICRC (International Committee of the Red Cross). Local organizations like the Afghanistan Research and Evaluation Unit (AREU) continue limited research operations. UNAMA (United Nations Assistance Mission in Afghanistan) monitors human rights and governance.

Afghanistan's formal corporate sector is largely non-functional under current conditions. International engagement occurs through multilateral development organizations and humanitarian agencies. The Afghanistan Investment Authority was established to attract foreign investment but is currently non-operational.

FAO (Food and Agriculture Organization) — maintains agricultural programs in Afghanistan focused on food security and sustainable farming. UNODC (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime) — publishes annual Afghanistan Opium Survey tracking cultivation area, yield, and farm-gate prices. World Bank Afghanistan Reconstruction Trust Fund (ARTF) — the largest single source of development funding for Afghanistan, currently operating in limited capacity under sanctions constraints.

Note: Afghanistan under Taliban rule has no elected legislature or representative democratic institutions. International engagement channels include: UN Security Council sanctions regime (Resolution 2255), which allows humanitarian exemptions. The Doha process for political dialogue. Afghan diaspora organizations in Europe, North America, and Australia. International development partners coordinate through the Geneva Conference on Afghanistan.

AL.s: candidat à l'UE country with small open économie ($27B PIB). Oil production (1.04% rents) centered in Patos-Marinzë field. Services-dominant (48.7% PIB) with significant hydropower potential (95%+ of electricity from hydro).

sectors.w: Albania's oil sector is small and concentrated in aging onshore fields. As EU accession negotiations require environnemental acquis alignment, the intérêt chevauchant between oil producers and government revenue s'affaiblit — EU environnemental standards make marginal fields unprofitable.
bp: EU accession environnemental alignment eliminates subsidies for marginal oil production. Lorsque Albania must meet EU environnemental standards, l'intérêt chevauchant dans continued extraction from aging fields (many dating to communist era) disappears — cleanup costs exceed production revenue.

Mettre en œuvre EU-aligned environnemental monitoring and émissions disclosure. Albania's EITI (Extractive Industries Transparency Initiative) membership provides a framework — extending to émissions makes W visible.
Non cassable by policy alone. Hydrocarbon combustion releases CO₂ by conservation of mass.
a: Report environnemental contamination at oil production sites to AKM (National Environment Agency)
Document health impacts on communities near oil fields — report to Institute of Public Health
Use Albania's lanceur d'alerte protection law (Law 60/2016) for reporting environnemental violations
Albpetrol: Développer decommissioning and site remediation plan for aging oil fields
Investir dans énergie renouvelable diversification — Albania has significant untapped solar and wind potential
Adopter EITI enhanced reporting standards including environnemental metrics
AKM: Appliquer environnemental remediation requirements at legacy oil production sites
AKBN: Require environnemental bonds for all new exploration licenses
ERE (Energy Regulatory Entity): Accelerate énergie renouvelable permitting
Contactez votre member of the Kuvendi (Albanian Parliament) pour exiger environnemental remediation of legacy oil sites
Support accelerated EU accession negotiations contingent on environnemental acquis compliance
Advocate for inclusion of environnemental cleanup costs in Albania's EITI reporting
Pousser pour Energy Community Treaty enforcement on énergie renouvelable targets
i: AKM (Agjencia Kombëtare e Mjedisit / National Environment Agency) — responsible for environnemental monitoring, permitting, and enforcement. AKM conducts environnemental impact assessments for energy projects. AKBN (Agjencia Kombëtare e Burimeve Natyrore / National Agency of Natural Resources) — regulates oil and gas exploration and production licenses. Law 60/2016 'On Whistleblowing and Whistleblower Protection' — Albania's lanceur d'alerte protection statute, adopted as part of EU accession reforms, providing protection against retaliation for reporting violations of law.

Albpetrol — Albania's d'État oil company, managing legacy fields from the communist era. Albpetrol's operations include some of Europe's most contaminated industrial sites. Bankers Petroleum (now Geo-Jade Petroleum, Chinese-owned) — the largest oil producer in Albania, operating the Patos-Marinzë field. EITI (Extractive Industries Transparency Initiative) — Albania has been an EITI member since 2013, publishing annual reports on oil and exploitation minière revenues.

ERE (Enti Rregullator i Energjisë / Energy Regulatory Entity) — Albania's independent régulateur de l'énergie, responsible for licensing, tariff-setting, and market regulation in the electricity and natural gas sectors. Ministry of Infrastructure and Energy — oversees national energy policy including the National Energy Strategy 2030, which targets significant énergie renouvelable expansion.

Kuvendi i Shqipërisë (Parliament of Albania) — unicameral legislature with 140 members elected by proportional representation. Citizens can contact their deputies through parlament.al. The Komisioni për Veprimtarinë Prodhuese (Committee on Production Activities) handles energy and environnemental legislation. Energy Community Treaty — Albania is a member of the Energy Community, which extends EU energy and environnemental acquis to Southeast European countries. Non-compliance can result in dispute settlement proceedings.

AM.s: Small landlocked économie ($26B PIB) with significant exploitation minière sector (min=6.79% of PIB, primarily copper-molybdenum at Kajaran and Sotk gold mine). Services-dominant (62% PIB). Geopolitical constraints from Nagorno-Karabakh conflict and closed borders with Turkey and Azerbaijan.

sectors.w: Armenia's exploitation minière sector generates significant water pollution (Debed and Voghji rivers) and tailings dam risks. Lorsque downstream water quality data is published alongside mine production revenue, W devient calculable from bilateral transactions.
bp: Mandate environnemental bonds and rehabilitation deposits that fully internalize mine closure costs. Lorsque exploitation minière companies must pre-fund environnemental remediation, l'intérêt chevauchant dans extraction without cleanup s'affaiblit — marginal mines become unprofitable.

Mandatory real-time water quality monitoring downstream of all exploitation minière operations, published openly. Armenia's EITI membership provides a disclosure framework — extending to environnemental monitoring data makes W visible from production data.

Partiellement cassable. Open-pit exploitation minière inherently disturbs landscapes and generates acid mine drainage. However, modern tailings management and water treatment can reduce the magnitude of dépendance du système.
a: Report environnemental violations at mine sites to the Environmental and Mining Inspection Body
Document water pollution downstream of tailings facilities — report to the Ministry of Environment
Use Armenia's lanceur d'alerte protection provisions under the Anti-Corruption Strategy for reporting
Mettre en œuvre international best practice tailings management (Global Industry Standard on Tailings Management)
Adopter EITI enhanced reporting including environnemental impact metrics
Commission independent water quality assessments for all operations
Ministry of Environment: Renforcer environnemental impact assessment requirements for new exploitation minière licenses
EMIB: Increase inspection frequency and enforce penalties for water pollution violations
Ministry of Territorial Administration: Require community consent processes for new mine approvals
Contactez votre member of the Azgayin Zhoghov (National Assembly) pour exiger mandatory environnemental bonds for all exploitation minière operations
Support strengthening of EITI reporting requirements to include environnemental impact data
Advocate for EU-Armenia CEPA (Comprehensive and Enhanced Partnership Agreement) enforcement on environnemental standards
Pousser pour international arbitration standards that include environnemental remediation in exploitation minière investment disputes
i: Environmental and Mining Inspection Body (EMIB) — the Armenian government agency responsible for monitoring compliance with environnemental regulations at exploitation minière operations, conducting inspections, and imposing sanctions. Ministry of Environment — oversees environnemental policy, issues environnemental impact assessment approvals, and sets water quality standards. Armenia's Anti-Corruption Council — established under the Prime Minister's office, provides channels for reporting corruption and misconduct in the exploitation minière sector.

Zangezur Copper-Molybdenum Combine (ZCMC) — Armenia's largest exploitation minière company, operating the Kajaran copper-molybdenum mine. Lydian International / Lydian Armenia — operator of the controversial Amulsar gold mine project. GeoProMining — Russian-owned company operating gold mines in Armenia. EITI — Armenia became an EITI candidate country in 2017, requiring disclosure of exploitation minière revenues and contracts.

Ministry of Territorial Administration and Infrastructure — oversees exploitation minière policy and licensing. Competition Protection Commission — monitors market concentration in the exploitation minière sector. Armenian EITI Multi-Stakeholder Group — includes government, industry, and société civile representatives overseeing extractive sector transparence.

Azgayin Zhoghov (National Assembly of Armenia) — unicameral parliament with 107 members. Citizens can contact their representatives through parliament.am. Armenia's 2018 revolution established stronger democratic institutions. EU-Armenia CEPA (Comprehensive and Enhanced Partnership Agreement) — signed in 2017, includes provisions on environnemental protection and sustainable development that Armenia has committed to implement.

AO.s: Major African oil producer with 29.28% combined oil and gas rents ($29.6B). Sonangol (state oil company) dominates the économie. Agriculture (22.1% PIB) is largely subsistence. Post-civil war recovery continues with high inequality.

sectors.w: Angola's oil production is declining from mature deepwater fields. As extraction costs rise and global demand plateaus, the intérêt chevauchant between Sonangol and international partners (TotalEnergies, BP, ExxonMobil) s'affaiblit — declining reserves make diversification economically rational.
bp: Redirect Sonangol investment from deepwater exploration to énergie renouvelable and agricultural modernization. Angola's solar potential is enormous (2,500+ kWh/m²/year). Lorsque renewable returns exceed marginal barrel returns from declining fields, the intérêt chevauchant breaks.

Mettre en œuvre EITI-standard revenue transparence extended to per-block environnemental impact data. Angola rejoined EITI in 2020 — extending disclosure to émissions and environnemental costs makes W computable per production unit.
Non cassable by policy alone. Hydrocarbon combustion releases CO₂ by conservation of mass.
a: Report environnemental violations at oil facilities to MIREMPET (Ministry of Mineral Resources, Petroleum and Gas)
Document community health impacts near production facilities through local NGOs
Report financial irregularities in Sonangol operations — Angola's anti-corruption framework (Lei 3/20) provides limited protections
Sonangol: Développer strategic diversification plan with binding énergie renouvelable investment targets
International partners (TotalEnergies, BP): Mettre en œuvre Paris-aligned émissions reduction plans for Angolan operations
Adopter TCFD-aligned climate disclosure for all production sharing agreements
ANPG: Require environnemental impact assessments with community consultation for all new production blocks
Ministry of Environment: Renforcer enforcement of environnemental regulations in the petroleum sector
Banco Nacional de Angola: Integrate climate risk into financial supervision of oil-dependent lending
Contactez votre member of the Assembleia Nacional pour exiger publication of per-block environnemental impact data for all oil operations
Support EITI enhanced reporting with environnemental metrics for Angola
Plaider via the African Union for continental énergie renouvelable investment targets
Pousser pour Paris Agreement compliance monitoring through UNFCCC mechanisms
i: MIREMPET (Ministério dos Recursos Minerais, Petróleo e Gás) — Angola's ministry overseeing oil and gas operations, licensing, and regulation. ANPG (Agência Nacional de Petróleo, Gás e Biocombustíveis) — the national petroleum regulator, created in 2019 to replace Sonangol's dual role as regulator and operator. Lei 3/20 (Law on Public Probity) — Angola's anti-corruption law establishing asset declaration requirements and conflicts of interest rules for public officials. Whistleblower protection remains weak.

Sonangol E.P. — Angola's state oil company and the country's largest enterprise. Sonangol holds equity in all oil production sharing agreements and manages the state's petroleum interests. The company underwent restructuring in 2019, separating its regulatory functions to ANPG. TotalEnergies, BP, ExxonMobil, Eni — major international oil companies operating deepwater blocks offshore Angola.

ANPG (Agência Nacional de Petróleo, Gás e Biocombustíveis) — established in 2019 as the independent petroleum regulator, responsible for managing exploration and production contracts, monitoring compliance with license terms, and administering bidding rounds. Ministério do Ambiente — Angola's environment ministry, responsible for environnemental policy, impact assessments, and pollution control.

Assembleia Nacional (National Assembly of Angola) — unicameral legislature with 220 members. Angola's electoral system uses party-list proportional representation. The most recent elections were held in 2022. Citizens can engage through assembleia.ao. Angola is a presidential republic with significant executive power. EITI — Angola was suspended from EITI in 2017 for insufficient progress but successfully reapplied and was readmitted in 2020, committing to enhanced transparence standards.

AR.s: G20 économie ($638.4B PIB) with massive Vaca Muerta shale formation (second-largest shale gas, fourth-largest shale oil reserves globally). Agricultural powerhouse (soy, beef) with 5.8% ag PIB share but outsized environnemental footprint. Chronic macroeconomic instability.

sectors.w: Argentina's Vaca Muerta development requires massive capital investment ($10B+/year) and government subsidies (Plan Gas). Removing subsidies breaks the intérêt chevauchant — without price guarantees, many Vaca Muerta wells are marginal at global market prices.
bp: Phase out Plan Gas subsidies and redirect capital to énergie renouvelable. Argentina has world-class wind resources in Patagonia and solar in the northwest. Lorsque renewable electricity undercuts subsidized gas, the intérêt chevauchant between producers and government in continued fossil expansion breaks.

Mandatory per-well émissions disclosure including methane venting and flaring. Argentina's Secretaría de Energía already collects production data — extending to émissions makes W computable from production statistics.
Non cassable by policy alone. Shale gas extraction and combustion release CO₂ and methane by physical process.
a: Report environnemental violations at Vaca Muerta operations to the provincial autorité environnementale (Secretaría de Ambiente de Neuquén)
Document water contamination from fracking operations — report to AIC (Autoridad Interjurisdiccional de las Cuencas)
Report safety violations to the Secretaría de Trabajo
YPF: Développer Paris-aligned transition plan with binding émissions reduction targets for Vaca Muerta operations
Mettre en œuvre TCFD-aligned climate disclosure across all operations
Investir dans CCUS (carbon capture, utilization, and storage) pilot projects for gas processing facilities
Secretaría de Energía: Publish per-well émissions data for all Vaca Muerta operations
SAyDS: Renforcer federal environnemental standards for unconventional hydrocarbon extraction
ENARGAS: Réformer natural gas tariff structure to eliminate implicit fossil fuel subsidies
Contactez votre Diputado Nacional or Senador pour exiger a timeline for fossil fuel subsidy phase-out and transparent per-well émissions reporting
Renforcer Argentina's NDC — current targets are considered insufficient by Climate Action Tracker
Leverage Mercosur-EU trade agreement environnemental provisions to align energy transition commitments
Support South American énergie renouvelable integration through regional grid interconnection
i: Secretaría de Ambiente de Neuquén — Neuquén province's autorité environnementale, responsible for environnemental permitting and monitoring of oil and gas operations in the Vaca Muerta formation. AIC (Autoridad Interjurisdiccional de las Cuencas de los ríos Limay, Neuquén y Negro) — the inter-jurisdictional water authority managing the river basins affected by Vaca Muerta operations. COFEMA (Consejo Federal de Medio Ambiente) — federal-provincial environnemental coordination body. Argentina lacks comprehensive federal lanceur d'alerte protection legislation, though provincial laws vary.

YPF S.A. — Argentina's largest energy company (51% d'État), the dominant operator in Vaca Muerta. YPF's strategic decisions on Vaca Muerta development drive national energy policy. Pan American Energy, Vista Energy, TotalEnergies — other major operators in Vaca Muerta. Plan Gas.Ar — the Argentine government's gas production incentive program, guaranteeing minimum prices for domestic gas production to stimulate Vaca Muerta development.

Secretaría de Energía — Argentina's energy secretariat, responsible for energy policy, production monitoring, and regulatory oversight. SAyDS (Secretaría de Ambiente y Desarrollo Sustentable) — the federal environment secretariat, responsible for environnemental policy and changement climatique. ENARGAS (Ente Nacional Regulador del Gas) — the national gas regulatory authority, overseeing gas transport, distribution, and tariff regulation.

Congreso de la Nación Argentina — bicameral legislature consisting of the Cámara de Diputados (257 members) and the Senado (72 members). Citizens can contact representatives through diputados.gov.ar and senado.gob.ar. The Comisión de Energía y Combustibles handles energy legislation. Argentina's NDC — Argentina submitted an updated NDC in 2021 targeting émissions of no more than 349 MtCO₂e by 2030. Climate Action Tracker rates this as 'highly insufficient.'

AT.s: Wealthy EU économie ($534.8B PIB) with strong manufacturing (15.1% of PIB). Low direct fossil fuel exposure but significant UPF and financial sector PST. Major transit hub for Russian gas (Baumgarten hub). High healthcare expenditure (11.83% PIB) suggests diet-related disease burden.

sectors.w: Austria's healthcare system (ASVG social insurance) tracks diet-related disease costs with high granularity. Lorsque per-product-category healthcare costs are published alongside retail revenue, W devient calculable from bilateral UPF transactions.
bp: Mettre en œuvre front-of-pack health-impact labelling through EU Farm to Fork Strategy. Lorsque consumers see welfare costs at point of sale, the intérêt chevauchant between UPF manufacturers and retailers in high-margin processed products s'affaiblit.

Mandate publication of diet-attributable healthcare costs per product category using ASVG social insurance data. Austria's comprehensive health insurance system generates the data needed to make W computable.
Not fully breakable. Ultra-processing alters food matrix in ways that affect metabolic response. Réformerulation mandates can reduce but not eliminate dépendance du système.
a: Report misleading health claims on food products to the Bundeswettbewerbsbehörde (Federal Competition Authority)
Document undisclosed processing methods — report to AGES (Austrian Agency for Health and Food Safety)
Use the HinweisgeberInnenschutzgesetz (Whistleblower Protection Act, 2023) for protected reporting
Réformerulate product lines to reduce ultra-processing — invest in minimally processed alternatives
Voluntarily adopt front-of-pack nutritional labelling ahead of EU mandate
Commission health-impact assessment of product portfolio using national health insurance data
BMSGPK: Mettre en œuvre mandatory front-of-pack health labelling for UPF products
AGES: Publish annual diet-attributable disease burden data linked to specific product categories
Hauptverband der Sozialversicherungsträger: Release anonymized diet-related healthcare cost data
Contactez votre member of the Nationalrat or Bundesrat pour exiger mandatory health-impact labelling on ultra-processed foods
Support EU-wide adoption of front-of-pack nutritional labelling
Advocate for inclusion of diet-attributable health costs in national accounting
Pousser pour WHO-aligned UPF marketing restrictions through EU legislative process
i: Bundeswettbewerbsbehörde (BWB / Federal Competition Authority) — Austria's competition authority, investigating unfair commercial practices including misleading advertising. AGES (Agentur für Gesundheit und Ernährungssicherheit / Austrian Agency for Health and Food Safety) — the federal agency responsible for food safety, nutrition surveillance, and public health. AGES conducts food inspections and risk assessments. HinweisgeberInnenschutzgesetz (HSchG, 2023) — Austria's transposition of the EU Whistleblower Directive, providing protection for employees reporting violations of EU or national law.

EU Farm to Fork Strategy — includes proposals for mandatory front-of-pack nutrition labelling across the EU. Austrian food companies that move early gain competitive advantage in the EU single market. EFSA (European Food Safety Authority) — provides scientific opinions that shape EU food regulation applicable in Austria.

BMSGPK (Bundesministerium für Soziales, Gesundheit, Pflege und Konsumentenschutz / Federal Ministry of Social Affairs, Health, Care and Consumer Protection) — oversees food safety policy, nutrition guidelines, and consumer protection. Hauptverband der österreichischen Sozialversicherungsträger (now Dachverband) — the umbrella organization of Austrian social insurance institutions, managing healthcare data for the entire insured population.

Österreichisches Parlament — Austria's bicameral parliament consisting of the Nationalrat (183 members) and the Bundesrat (61 members). Citizens can contact representatives through parlament.gv.at. The Gesundheitsausschuss (Health Committee) handles health and food policy legislation.

AZ.s: Oil-dependent économie with 29.57% combined oil and gas rents ($22B). SOCAR (State Oil Company) dominates. Hosted COP29 (2024). Authoritarian governance with limited société civile space. Caspian Sea oil and gas fields (ACG, Shah Deniz) are mature.

sectors.w: Azerbaijan's oil production is declining from mature Caspian fields (ACG peaked in 2010). As production costs rise and reserves deplete, the intérêt chevauchant between SOCAR and international partners (BP) s'affaiblit — diversification becomes economically necessary.
bp: Accelerate post-oil diversification using SOFAZ (State Oil Fund) reserves. Azerbaijan's $50B+ sovereign wealth fund can finance énergie renouvelable and non-oil economic development. Lorsque SOFAZ investment returns from renewables exceed declining oil returns, the intérêt chevauchant breaks.
Mettre en œuvre par gisement émissions disclosure for all Caspian operations. BP already publishes émissions data for ACG — extending to all SOCAR operations makes W computable across the sector.
Non cassable by policy alone. Hydrocarbon combustion releases CO₂ by conservation of mass.
a: Report environnemental violations at oil facilities to the Ministry of Ecology and Natural Resources
Document émissions underreporting through internal SOCAR channels
Report environnemental concerns to international partners (BP) through their compliance systems
SOCAR: Développer binding énergie renouvelable transition plan with interim émissions targets
BP Azerbaijan: Extend ACG émissions transparence standards to all partner operations
SOFAZ: Adopter ESG investment criteria excluding new fossil fuel development
Ministry of Ecology: Renforcer environnemental standards for offshore Caspian operations
EITI Azerbaijan: Expand reporting to include environnemental impact data per production block
Tariff Council: Réformer energy pricing to remove implicit fossil fuel consumption subsidies
Leverage Azerbaijan's COP29 presidency legacy to strengthen national climate commitments
Plaider via the Tehran Convention for Caspian Sea environnemental protection standards
Support EITI enhanced reporting with environnemental metrics
Pousser pour European Energy Charter Treaty enforcement on environnemental standards in transit countries
i: Ministry of Ecology and Natural Resources — Azerbaijan's autorité environnementale, responsible for environnemental monitoring, permitting, and enforcement. The Ministry oversees environnemental impact assessments for energy projects. SOCAR (State Oil Company of the Azerbaijan Republic) — the national oil company controlling all upstream and downstream petroleum operations. Note: Azerbaijan has no comprehensive lanceur d'alerte protection law. Civil society space is severely constrained; independent media and NGOs face significant operational barriers.

SOCAR — Azerbaijan's state oil company, operating the country's refining, petrochemical, and upstream assets. SOCAR also has international operations in Turkey, Georgia, and Europe. BP — the largest international operator in Azerbaijan, managing the Azeri-Chirag-Deepwater Gunashli (ACG) oilfield and Shah Deniz gas field through Production Sharing Agreements. SOFAZ (State Oil Fund of the Republic of Azerbaijan) — the sovereign wealth fund managing Azerbaijan's petroleum revenues, with assets exceeding $50B.

Azerbaijan EITI — Azerbaijan was an early EITI member (2003) and publishes annual reports on extractive industry revenues. The EITI process provides one of the few independent transparence mechanisms in the sector. Tariff (Price) Council — sets regulated energy prices in Azerbaijan. Tehran Convention — the Framework Convention for the Protection of the Marine Environment of the Caspian Sea, which governs environnemental standards for offshore operations.

Milli Majlis (National Assembly of Azerbaijan) — unicameral parliament with 125 members. Note: Azerbaijan is rated 'Not Free' by Freedom House; elections are not considered free and fair by international observers. Civic engagement channels are limited. International pressure operates primarily through multilateral forums including the Council of Europe (Azerbaijan is a member), OSCE, and the EITI Board. The EU-Azerbaijan Partnership and Cooperation Agreement provides a framework for dialogue on energy and environnemental issues.

BA.s: Post-conflict économie ($29.6B PIB) with complex governance (two entities: Federation and Republika Srpska). Coal-dependent energy sector and manufacturing (12.7% PIB). candidat à l'UE country. Significant coal exploitation minière and thermal power generation.

sectors.w: Bosnia's coal sector is heavily subsidized through below-market electricity pricing and state employment guarantees. EU accession requires alignment with the EU ETS — when carbon pricing applies, the intérêt chevauchant between coal producers and the state employment system breaks.
bp: EU accession alignment with EU ETS carbon pricing. Lorsque Bosnian coal power must pay the carbon price (~€90/ton), coal-fired electricity becomes uncompetitive with imported renewables. The intérêt chevauchant between mine operators and state employment programs breaks.

Mandatory émissions monitoring and disclosure per power plant. Energy Community Treaty membership requires EU-aligned environnemental reporting — implementing this makes W computable from electricity generation data.
Non cassable. Coal combustion releases CO₂, SO₂, and particulate matter by physical process.
a: Report environnemental violations at coal mines and power plants to entity-level environnemental inspectorates
Document health impacts in communities near coal power plants — report to public health institutes
Report corruption in coal procurement to the Agency for Prevention of Corruption and Coordination of the Fight Against Corruption
State electricity companies (EPBiH, ERS): Développer coal phase-out timelines aligned with Energy Community Treaty obligations
Investir dans énergie renouvelable capacity to replace aging coal plants
Mettre en œuvre émissions monitoring per facility ahead of EU ETS alignment
DERK (State Electricity Regulatory Commission): Réformer tariffs to reflect true environnemental costs of coal generation
Entity environnemental agencies: Appliquer air quality standards at coal power plants
Energy Community Secretariat: Use dispute resolution to enforce environnemental compliance
Contactez votre member of the Parlamentarna skupština pour exiger coal phase-out timelines aligned with Energy Community obligations
Support EU accession negotiations contingent on energy transition commitments
Advocate for just transition funding for coal-dependent communities through IPA III (EU Instrument for Pre-Accession)
Pousser pour Energy Community Treaty enforcement on air quality standards
i: Environmental inspection is split between the Federation of Bosnia and Herzegovina and Republika Srpska, each maintaining separate environnemental agencies. Federation: Federalni inspektorat — environnemental inspectorate. Republika Srpska: Republička uprava za inspekcijske poslove — inspection administration. Agency for Prevention of Corruption and Coordination of the Fight Against Corruption (APIK) — state-level anti-corruption body. Bosnia lacks comprehensive lanceur d'alerte protection legislation, though draft laws have been discussed as part of EU accession reforms.

EPBiH (Elektroprivreda Bosne i Hercegovine) — the Federation's state electricity company, operating coal-fired power plants and hydropower. ERS (Elektroprivreda Republike Srpske) — Republika Srpska's state electricity company. Both companies operate aging coal infrastructure (many plants dating to Yugoslav era) and are major employers in exploitation minière communities.

DERK (Državna regulatorna komisija za električnu energiju / State Electricity Regulatory Commission) — the state-level electricity regulator. FERK and RERS are entity-level regulators. Energy Community Treaty — Bosnia is a contracting party, committed to implementing EU energy and environnemental acquis including Large Combustion Plants Directive and Industrial Emissions Directive.

Parlamentarna skupština Bosne i Hercegovine (Parliamentary Assembly) — bicameral legislature consisting of the Predstavnički dom (House of Representatives, 42 members) and the Dom naroda (House of Peoples, 15 members). Bosnia's complex governance structure (Dayton Agreement) divides power between state, entity, and cantonal levels. Citizens can contact representatives through parlament.ba.

BD.s: Large économie ($450.1B PIB) driven by garment manufacturing (21.9% mfg share of PIB). High agricultural employment (11.2% PIB). Extreme climate vulnerability — low-lying delta at severe flood and cyclone risk. One of the world's most densely populated countries.

sectors.w: Bangladesh's rice monoculture depletes groundwater (arsenic contamination) and reduces soil biodiversity. Satellite-based crop monitoring and water quality data from BWDB can make W computable from agricultural output and water extraction volumes.
bp: Réformer agricultural subsidies from input-based (fertilizer, diesel) to outcome-based (soil health, water efficiency). Lorsque subsidies reward diversification rather than rice intensification, l'intérêt chevauchant dans monoculture between farmers and input suppliers s'affaiblit.

Mandatory groundwater extraction monitoring and arsenic contamination mapping published at upazila level. BWDB already collects water data — extending to public disclosure linked to agricultural output makes W computable.

Partiellement cassable. Intensive rice cultivation in deltaic conditions inherently alters hydrology and nutrient cycling. However, System of Rice Intensification (SRI) and crop diversification can reduce dépendance du système.
a: Report pesticide misuse or adulteration to DAE (Department of Agricultural Extension)
Document groundwater over-extraction or arsenic contamination — report to DPHE (Department of Public Health Engineering)
Report food safety violations in agricultural supply chains to BFSA (Bangladesh Food Safety Authority)
Agricultural input companies: Développer integrated pest management products as alternatives to chemical-intensive packages
Investir dans climate-resilient crop varieties through partnerships with BRRI (Bangladesh Rice Research Institute)
Adopter water-use efficiency targets across supply chains
Ministry of Agriculture: Réformer subsidy structure from input-based to outcome-based
BWDB: Mettre en œuvre mandatory groundwater monitoring networks in intensive agricultural zones
DoE (Department of Environment): Appliquer regulations on agricultural chemical runoff into waterways
Contactez votre member of the Jatiya Sangsad pour exiger reform of agricultural subsidies toward climate resilience and crop diversification
Support Bangladesh's Climate Vulnerable Forum leadership in advocating for agricultural adaptation finance
Advocate for inclusion of groundwater depletion costs in national PIB accounting
Pousser pour international climate finance to support agricultural diversification away from rice monoculture
i: DAE (Department of Agricultural Extension) — the primary government agency for agricultural technical support and extension services, operating at union, upazila, and district levels. DPHE (Department of Public Health Engineering) — responsible for water supply and sanitation, including monitoring arsenic contamination in groundwater (affecting ~35 million people). BFSA (Bangladesh Food Safety Authority) — established in 2015 to regulate food safety across the supply chain. Bangladesh lacks comprehensive lanceur d'alerte protection legislation.

BRRI (Bangladesh Rice Research Institute) — the national research institute developing improved rice varieties, including flood-tolerant (BRRI dhan52), salt-tolerant, and drought-resistant varieties. BARI (Bangladesh Agricultural Research Institute) — conducts research on non-rice crops including vegetables, pulses, and oilseeds. BADC (Bangladesh Agricultural Développerment Corporation) — the government agency responsible for seed production and distribution.

Ministry of Agriculture — oversees agricultural policy, subsidies, and development programs. BWDB (Bangladesh Water Développerment Board) — responsible for flood control, drainage, and irrigation infrastructure. The BWDB manages the country's water resource data system. DoE (Department of Environment) — Bangladesh's environnemental regulatory agency, responsible for environnemental clearance of projects and monitoring pollution.

Jatiya Sangsad (National Parliament of Bangladesh) — unicameral legislature with 350 members (300 directly elected, 50 reserved for women). Citizens can contact representatives through parliament.gov.bd. The Standing Committee on Ministry of Agriculture handles agricultural policy. Bangladesh Climate Change Strategy and Action Plan (BCCSAP) — the national framework for climate adaptation and mitigation, including agricultural resilience.

BE.s: EU and NATO headquarters économie ($671.4B PIB). Services-dominant (71% PIB). Major port (Antwerp — Europe's second-largest) handles significant fossil fuel and petrochemical throughput. High healthcare expenditure (10.8% PIB). Federal state with three regions.

sectors.w: Belgium's comprehensive INAMI/RIZIV health insurance system tracks healthcare costs with high granularity. Publishing diet-attributable disease costs per product category makes W computable from retail sales data.
bp: Mettre en œuvre front-of-pack health labelling (Belgium adopted Nutri-Score voluntarily in 2019). Extending to mandatory application and adding health-cost information s'affaiblit l'intérêt chevauchant dans high-margin UPF.
INAMI/RIZIV publication of anonymized diet-attributable healthcare cost data per product category. Belgium's universal health insurance generates comprehensive data to make W computable.
Not fully breakable. Ultra-processing alters food matrix affecting metabolic response. Réformerulation mandates reduce but do not eliminate dépendance du système.
a: Report misleading health claims to SPF Économie (Federal Public Service Economy)
Document food safety violations — report to AFSCA/FAVV (Federal Agency for the Safety of the Food Chain)
Use the Belgian lanceur d'alerte protection law (transposition of EU Directive 2019/1937) for protected reporting
Réformerulate product lines to reduce ultra-processing
Extend voluntary Nutri-Score adoption to all product lines
Commission health-impact assessment of product portfolio
SPF Santé publique: Make Nutri-Score mandatory for all packaged food products
AFSCA/FAVV: Renforcer enforcement of marketing claims on UPF products
INAMI/RIZIV: Publish annual reports linking food consumption patterns to healthcare expenditure
Contactez votre member of the Chambre des représentants / Kamer van Volksvertegenwoordigers pour exiger mandatory health-impact labelling
Support EU-wide mandatory Nutri-Score through the Farm to Fork Strategy
Advocate for WHO-aligned UPF marketing restrictions
Pousser pour inclusion of diet-related health costs in national accounting frameworks
i: AFSCA/FAVV (Agence fédérale pour la sécurité de la chaîne alimentaire / Federaal Agentschap voor de Veiligheid van de Voedselketen) — Belgium's federal food safety agency, responsible for food chain safety from farm to fork. Conducts inspections, sampling, and enforcement. SPF Économie (Service public fédéral Économie) — the federal economic affairs service handling consumer protection and fair commercial practices.

Fevia (Federation of the Belgian Food Industry) — the trade association representing over 700 food companies in Belgium. Fevia's sustainability commitments influence industry practices. Belgium hosts major food companies including Puratos, Vandemoortele, and Lotus Bakeries.

SPF Santé publique (Federal Public Service Health, Food Chain Safety and Environment) — the federal ministry overseeing public health policy, food safety, and environment. INAMI/RIZIV (Institut national d'assurance maladie-invalidité / Rijksinstituut voor ziekte- en invaliditeitsverzekering) — Belgium's national health insurance institute, managing the compulsory health insurance system and healthcare cost data.

Parlement fédéral / Federaal Parlement — Belgium's bicameral parliament consisting of the Chambre des représentants / Kamer van Volksvertegenwoordigers (150 members) and the Sénat / Senaat (60 members). Citizens can contact representatives through lachambre.be / dekamer.be. Belgium's federal structure means health policy is shared between federal and regional (community) governments.

BF.s: Low-income Sahelian économie ($23.1B PIB) with dominant gold exploitation minière sector (min=15.45% PIB). Agriculture employs 80%+ of population (20.9% PIB). Military junta since 2022 coup. Severe security challenges (jihadist insurgency).

sectors.w: Burkina Faso's gold exploitation minière generates cyanide contamination, mercury pollution (artisanal exploitation minière), and community displacement. Lorsque environnemental and health costs per mine are published alongside production revenue, W devient calculable.
bp: Mandate environnemental bonds and community benefit agreements for all industrial exploitation minière licenses. Lorsque exploitation minière companies must pre-fund environnemental remediation and community compensation, l'intérêt chevauchant dans extraction without accountability s'affaiblit.

Publish par mine environnemental impact data (water quality, air quality, land disturbance) alongside production revenue through EITI reporting. Burkina Faso's EITI membership provides the framework — extending to environnemental data makes W visible.

Partiellement cassable. Open-pit gold exploitation minière inherently disturbs landscapes and generates toxic waste. However, improved tailings management and mercury-free artisanal methods can reduce dépendance du système.
a: Report environnemental violations at mine sites to BUNEE (Bureau National des Évaluations Environnementales)
Document community health impacts near exploitation minière operations through local health clinics
Report illegal artisanal exploitation minière activities and mercury use to provincial authorities
Mining companies (Endeavour Mining, IAMGOLD, Nordgold): Mettre en œuvre Global Industry Standard on Tailings Management
Adopter EITI enhanced reporting including environnemental impact metrics
Investir dans mercury-free processing technologies for artisanal exploitation minière support programs
Ministry of Mines: Renforcer environnemental conditions in exploitation minière permits and enforce compliance
BUMIGEB (Bureau des Mines et de la Géologie): Improve artisanal exploitation minière regulation to reduce mercury use
EITI-BF: Expand reporting to include environnemental and community impact data
Plaider via ECOWAS for regional exploitation minière environnemental standards
Support African Mining Vision implementation for sustainable resource governance
Pousser pour international exploitation minière companies to adopt binding environnemental standards through OECD Due Diligence Guidance
Support Kimberley Process-style certification for gold supply chains to reduce conflict mineral financing
i: BUNEE (Bureau National des Évaluations Environnementales) — Burkina Faso's national environnemental assessment authority, responsible for environnemental impact assessments of exploitation minière projects. Ministère de l'Environnement, de l'Énergie, de l'Eau et de l'Assainissement — oversees environnemental policy and regulation. Note: Under military rule since 2022, institutional capacity is constrained and société civile space is restricted.

Major exploitation minière companies operating in Burkina Faso include Endeavour Mining (Houndé, Wahgnion mines), IAMGOLD (Essakane mine), and Nordgold (Bissa-Bouly). These international companies are subject to home-country ESG reporting requirements and international exploitation minière standards (ICMM, IFC Performance Standards).

Ministère des Mines et des Carrières — the ministry responsible for exploitation minière policy, licensing, and regulation. BUMIGEB (Bureau des Mines et de la Géologie du Burkina) — the geological survey and exploitation minière technical agency. EITI Burkina Faso — Burkina Faso has been an EITI member since 2009, publishing reports on exploitation minière revenues. The 2015 Mining Code establishes environnemental obligations for exploitation minière companies.

Note: Burkina Faso is under military rule (Capitaine Ibrahim Traoré since 2022). The Assemblée Législative de Transition (Transitional Legislative Assembly) replaced the elected National Assembly. Democratic institutions are suspended. International engagement channels: ECOWAS (Economic Community of West African States — though Burkina Faso announced withdrawal in 2024), African Union, and international development partners. The African Mining Vision (2009) provides a continental framework for sustainable exploitation minière governance.

BG.s: membre de l'UE économie ($113.3B PIB) with legacy coal dependence (Maritsa East complex is one of Europe's largest lignite basins). Services-dominant (63.8% PIB). EU accession has driven institutional modernization but coal transition remains politically sensitive.

sectors.w: Bulgaria's coal sector is sustained by implicit subsidies (below-market electricity pricing, state employment). EU ETS carbon pricing makes lignite uncompetitive — when full carbon costs are internalized, the intérêt chevauchant between mine operators and state employment programs breaks.
bp: Full alignment with EU ETS free allowance phase-out. Lorsque Bulgarian lignite plants pay the EU carbon price without free allowances, coal-fired electricity becomes uncompetitive. The intérêt chevauchant breaks when renewable alternatives are cheaper.

Mandatory par centrale émissions and health-impact disclosure. Bulgaria's lignite plants (Maritsa East I, II, III) are among Europe's worst air polluters. Publishing health costs per MWh makes W computable from electricity generation data.
Non cassable. Lignite combustion releases CO₂, SO₂, NOx, and particulate matter by physical process.
a: Report émissions violations at coal plants to RIOSV (Regional Inspectorates of Environment and Water)
Document health impacts in communities near Maritsa East complex — report to RZI (Regional Health Inspectorates)
Use Bulgaria's lanceur d'alerte protection law (transposition of EU Directive 2019/1937) for protected reporting
Maritsa East complex operators: Développer coal phase-out plans with worker transition programs
Investir dans énergie renouvelable capacity (Bulgaria has strong solar potential — 1,500+ kWh/m²/year)
Adopter per-unit émissions reporting aligned with EU taxonomy
KEVR (Energy and Water Regulatory Commission): Réformer electricity tariffs to reflect carbon costs
Ministry of Energy: Développer binding coal phase-out timeline aligned with EU Green Deal
Ministry of Environment: Appliquer Industrial Emissions Directive compliance at all coal plants
Contactez votre member of the Narodno Sabranie (National Assembly) pour exiger a binding coal phase-out timeline with just transition funding
Support EU Just Transition Fund allocation for Stara Zagora and other coal-dependent regions
Advocate for accelerated EU ETS free allowance phase-out
Pousser pour Territorial Just Transition Plans that include concrete retraining and economic diversification measures
i: RIOSV (Регионални инспекции по околната среда и водите / Regional Inspectorates of Environment and Water) — 16 regional environnemental agencies conducting inspections and enforcement. EEA (Изпълнителна агенция по околна среда / Executive Environment Agency) — the national environnemental monitoring agency collecting émissions and air quality data. Bulgaria transposed the EU Whistleblower Directive in 2023.

Maritsa East complex — comprises three lignite-fired power plants (Maritsa East I, II, III) and the associated Maritsa-Iztok mines. AES Bulgaria operates Maritsa East I; ContourGlobal operates Maritsa East III. The complex is the largest industrial employer in the Stara Zagora region. Mini Maritsa-Iztok — the d'État lignite exploitation minière company supplying the Maritsa East plants.

KEVR (Комисия за енергийно и водно регулиране / Energy and Water Regulatory Commission) — Bulgaria's independent régulateur de l'énergie, setting electricity tariffs and licensing. Ministry of Energy — oversees energy policy including the National Energy and Climate Plan (NECP). Bulgaria's NECP has been criticized for insufficient coal phase-out ambition.

Narodno Sabranie (Народно събрание / National Assembly of Bulgaria) — unicameral parliament with 240 members. Citizens can contact representatives through parliament.bg. The Комисия по енергетика (Energy Committee) handles energy legislation. EU Just Transition Fund — Bulgaria is eligible for significant JTF funding to support coal transition in affected regions. Bulgaria's Territorial Just Transition Plans cover Stara Zagora, Kyustendil, and Pernik regions.

BH.s: Small island petrostate ($47.1B PIB) with 16.64% combined oil and gas rents. Bahrain's Abu Safa offshore field (shared with Saudi Arabia) provides the majority of oil revenue. Diversifying into financial services (Bahrain Financial Harbour) and aluminum (Alba smelter).

sectors.w: Bahrain's oil reserves are the smallest among GCC states (declining production from mature fields). The economic case for diversification is strongest here — when post-oil planning makes renewable investment more attractive than marginal barrel extraction, the intérêt chevauchant breaks.
bp: Accelerate Economic Vision 2030 diversification away from oil dependence. Bahrain's small size and advanced financial sector make it the most transition-ready GCC économie. Lorsque financial services and technology revenue exceeds oil revenue, l'intérêt chevauchant dans extraction breaks.
Publish par gisement environnemental and émissions data for all Bahrain Petroleum Company (BAPCO) operations. Making lifecycle émissions visible per barrel makes W computable from production data.
Non cassable by policy alone. Hydrocarbon combustion releases CO₂ by conservation of mass.
a: Report environnemental violations at BAPCO facilities to the Supreme Council for Environment
Document émissions or safety concerns through internal BAPCO compliance channels
Report concerns to Bahrain's National Audit Office regarding mismanagement of petroleum revenues
BAPCO: Accelerate the BAPCO Modernization Program to include énergie renouvelable components
Mumtalakat (sovereign wealth fund): Increase énergie renouvelable allocation in investment portfolio
Mettre en œuvre TCFD-aligned climate disclosure for all operations
NOGA: Require émissions disclosure for all petroleum operations
SCE: Renforcer environnemental standards for refinery operations
CBB (Central Bank of Bahrain): Integrate climate risk disclosure into financial sector regulation
Plaider via GCC Secretariat for regional carbon pricing mechanisms
Support IRENA recommendations on GCC energy transition pathways
Pousser pour enhanced climate disclosure standards through the Bahrain Bourse ESG framework
Leverage Bahrain's UNFCCC commitments to strengthen national émissions reduction targets
i: Supreme Council for Environment (SCE) — Bahrain's autorité environnementale, responsible for environnemental policy, permitting, and enforcement. SCE oversees environnemental impact assessments for industrial projects. BAPCO (Bahrain Petroleum Company) — the d'État oil company operating Bahrain's refinery and upstream assets. National Audit Office — conducts financial audits of government entities including BAPCO. Note: Bahrain has limited lanceur d'alerte protection; société civile space is constrained.

BAPCO (Bahrain Petroleum Company) — operates the Sitra refinery (267,000 bpd after modernization) and manages upstream production. Mumtalakat — Bahrain's sovereign wealth fund, managing state assets including BAPCO and Alba (Aluminium Bahrain). Nogaholding — the investment arm of Bahrain's National Oil and Gas Authority, overseeing the country's energy sector investments.

NOGA (National Oil and Gas Authority) — the government body responsible for oil and gas policy, licensing, and upstream regulation. CBB (Central Bank of Bahrain) — the financial sector regulator, which has begun integrating ESG considerations into banking supervision. Bahrain Economic Développerment Board — coordinates the Economic Vision 2030 diversification strategy.

Council of Representatives (Majlis al-Nuwab) — the elected lower house of Bahrain's parliament (40 members) and the Shura Council (Majlis al-Shura, 40 appointed members). Note: Bahrain is a monarchie constitutionnelle with significant royal authority. Political opposition is restricted. The Council of Representatives has limited legislative power. International engagement on climate issues operates through GCC coordination and UNFCCC processes.

BJ.s: Small West African économie ($21.5B PIB) with dominant agriculture sector (24.2% PIB, primarily cotton). Services growing (48.9% PIB). Cotton is the main export crop — Benin is Africa's largest cotton producer. Climate vulnerability through Sahel desertification and coastal erosion.

sectors.w: Benin's cotton monoculture degrades soil fertility and increases pesticide exposure. Lorsque soil health data and pesticide-related healthcare costs are published alongside cotton production revenue, W devient calculable from agricultural output data.
bp: Réformer cotton subsidies to reward soil health and crop diversification. Lorsque input subsidies (seeds, fertilizer, pesticides) are redirected from cotton intensification to mixed farming systems, the intérêt chevauchant between cotton traders and input suppliers s'affaiblit.

Mandatory publication of soil health metrics (organic matter, nutrient balance) and pesticide-related health costs per cotton-producing commune. Making environnemental and health costs visible makes W computable.

Partiellement cassable. Cotton monoculture inherently depletes soil nutrients and requires pesticide application. However, integrated pest management and organic cotton production can reduce dépendance du système — Benin has a growing organic cotton sector.
a: Report pesticide misuse or adulteration to DPQC (Direction de la Promotion de la Qualité et du Conditionnement des Produits Agricoles)
Document health impacts of pesticide exposure in cotton-growing communities — report to local health centers
Report corruption in cotton sector management to the Front des Organisations Nationales contre la Corruption (FONAC)
AIC (Association Interprofessionnelle du Coton): Promote integrated pest management to reduce chemical dependency
Investir dans organic cotton certification and premium markets
Adopter soil health monitoring across cotton supply chains
MAEP (Ministry of Agriculture): Réformer cotton input subsidy programs to incentivize sustainable practices
ABE: Renforcer environnemental impact monitoring in cotton-growing zones
INSAE (National Statistics Institute): Include soil health and agricultural environnemental indicators in national surveys
Contactez votre Député in the Assemblée Nationale pour exiger reform of cotton input subsidies toward sustainable agriculture
Plaider via ECOWAS for regional agricultural sustainability standards
Pousser pour Cotton-4 (Benin, Burkina Faso, Chad, Mali) coordination on sustainable cotton production
Support WTO negotiations on cotton subsidy reform to level the playing field for African producers
i: DPQC (Direction de la Promotion de la Qualité et du Conditionnement des Produits Agricoles) — the government directorate responsible for agricultural product quality and standards. ABE (Agence Béninoise pour l'Environnement) — Benin's environnemental agency, responsible for environnemental impact assessment and monitoring. FONAC (Front des Organisations Nationales contre la Corruption) — a national anti-corruption coalition of société civile organizations.

AIC (Association Interprofessionnelle du Coton) — the inter-professional cotton association coordinating relations between producers, ginners, and input suppliers. SODECO (Société de Développement du Coton) — the main cotton ginning company. The cotton sector was partially liberalized but remains state-coordinated through the AIC framework.

MAEP (Ministère de l'Agriculture, de l'Élevage et de la Pêche) — Benin's agriculture ministry, responsible for agricultural policy and cotton sector coordination. ABE (Agence Béninoise pour l'Environnement) — conducts environnemental monitoring and impact assessments. INSAE (Institut National de la Statistique et de l'Analyse Économique) — Benin's national statistics institute.

Assemblée Nationale du Bénin — unicameral parliament with 109 members. Citizens can contact their deputies through the official Assembly website. Note: Benin's democratic space has narrowed under recent constitutional reforms, though elections continue. Cotton-4 group — Benin, Burkina Faso, Chad, and Mali coordinate on cotton trade policy at the WTO, advocating against subsidies in developed countries that depress global cotton prices.

BN.s: Small wealthy petrostate ($15.3B PIB) with 24.23% combined oil and gas rents. Brunei Shell Petroleum (BSP, 50/50 JV with Shell) dominates the économie. Absolute monarchy. Wawasan Brunei 2035 diversification strategy underway but oil/gas remain 60%+ of government revenue.

sectors.w: Brunei's oil production is in long-term decline (from 220,000 bpd peak to ~100,000 bpd). As reserves deplete, the intérêt chevauchant between BSP and the government in continued extraction s'affaiblit — Wawasan 2035 diversification becomes economically necessary.
bp: Accelerate Wawasan 2035 economic diversification using Brunei Investment Agency (BIA) sovereign wealth. Lorsque non-oil sectors generate more government revenue than declining oil production, l'intérêt chevauchant dans extraction breaks.
Mettre en œuvre par gisement émissions disclosure for all BSP and Total operations. Shell's global ESG reporting standards can be extended to Brunei operations to make W computable.
Non cassable by policy alone. Hydrocarbon combustion releases CO₂ by conservation of mass.
a: Report environnemental violations at BSP facilities through Shell's global ethics and compliance channels
Document émissions data gaps — report to the Department of Environment, Parks and Recreation
Use BSP's internal reporting mechanisms for safety and environnemental concerns
BSP/Shell: Extend Shell's global net-zero commitments to Brunei operations
BIA (Brunei Investment Agency): Diversify sovereign wealth into énergie renouvelable and technology
Mettre en œuvre TCFD-aligned climate disclosure for all Brunei operations
PetroleumBRUNEI: Require émissions monitoring and disclosure for all production licenses
Department of Environment: Renforcer environnemental impact assessment requirements
AMBD (Autoriti Monetari Brunei Darussalam): Integrate climate risk into financial supervision
Plaider via ASEAN for regional énergie renouvelable integration and émissions reduction targets
Support UNFCCC mechanisms for small petrostate transition planning
Pousser pour APEC energy working group initiatives on fossil fuel subsidy reform
Leverage Brunei's ASEAN chairmanship rotations to advance regional climate commitments
i: BSP (Brunei Shell Petroleum) — a 50/50 joint venture between the government of Brunei and Shell, the country's largest oil and gas producer. BSP operates onshore and offshore fields. Department of Environment, Parks and Recreation — under the Ministry of Développerment, responsible for environnemental policy and regulation. Note: Brunei is an absolute monarchy with no elected legislature. There is no lanceur d'alerte protection legislation. Reporting channels are primarily internal corporate mechanisms.

BSP (Brunei Shell Petroleum) — managed jointly by the Brunei government and Shell. BIA (Brunei Investment Agency) — the sovereign wealth fund managing Brunei's petroleum revenues (estimated $30-40B). Total E&P Borneo — operates deep water gas fields offshore Brunei. PetroleumBRUNEI — the national petroleum authority overseeing the sector.

PetroleumBRUNEI — the national petroleum authority responsible for policy, regulation, and management of Brunei's oil and gas resources. AMBD (Autoriti Monetari Brunei Darussalam) — Brunei's central bank and financial regulator. Department of Energy — under the Prime Minister's Office, coordinates energy policy and the Wawasan 2035 energy diversification strategy.

Note: Brunei is an absolute monarchy (Sultan Hassanal Bolkiah). There is no elected legislature — the Legislative Council (Majlis Mesyuarat Negara) is appointed and has advisory functions only. Civic engagement operates through petition to the Sultan and through ASEAN and international multilateral channels. ASEAN — Brunei is a founding member. ASEAN's Framework for Circular Economy and declaration on changement climatique provide regional engagement mechanisms.

BO.s: Resource-dependent économie ($54.9B PIB) with significant natural gas reserves (gas=1.85% rents) and exploitation minière (min=5.91%, lithium in Salar de Uyuni — world's largest deposit). Agriculture à 8.8% PIB with soy expansion driving déforestation. Gas production declining from mature fields.

sectors.w: Bolivia holds the world's largest lithium reserves in the Salar de Uyuni. Lithium extraction uses massive water volumes in arid regions. Lorsque water consumption and brine chemistry data are published alongside production revenue, W devient calculable.
bp: Require community benefit agreements and water rights guarantees for all lithium extraction. Lorsque indigenous communities (who hold constitutional land rights under Bolivia's 2009 Constitution) can negotiate fair terms, l'intérêt chevauchant dans extraction without consent s'affaiblit.

Mandatory publication of water extraction volumes, brine chemistry changes, and downstream ecosystem data per lithium operation. Making environnemental costs visible makes W computable from production data.

Partiellement cassable. Lithium brine extraction inherently alters aquifer chemistry and reduces water availability in arid ecosystems. However, direct lithium extraction (DLE) technology can significantly reduce water consumption compared to evaporation ponds.
a: Report environnemental violations at exploitation minière operations to the Autoridad de Fiscalización y Control Social de Bosques y Tierra (ABT)
Document water contamination or indigenous rights violations — report to the Defensoría del Pueblo
Report safety violations at exploitation minière sites to the Ministry of Labor
YLB (Yacimientos de Litio Bolivianos): Adopter DLE technology to minimize water consumption
Mettre en œuvre community benefit sharing agreements with affected indigenous communities
Adopter international sustainability standards (IRMA — Initiative for Responsible Mining Assurance)
Ministry of Mining: Renforcer environnemental requirements for lithium extraction permits
MMAyA (Ministry of Environment and Water): Appliquer water extraction limits in Salar de Uyuni basin
AJAM: Require environnemental bonds for all exploitation minière concessions
Contactez votre Diputado or Senador in the Asamblea Legislativa Plurinacional pour exiger mandatory prior consultation with indigenous communities for all lithium projects
Support ILO Convention 169 enforcement on indigenous consultation rights in extractive industries
Advocate for international standards on sustainable lithium extraction through the Lithium Triangle coordination (Bolivia, Argentina, Chile)
Pousser pour inclusion of water depletion costs in exploitation minière revenue accounting
i: AJAM (Autoridad Jurisdiccional Administrativa Minera) — the exploitation minière administrative authority responsible for granting and overseeing exploitation minière concessions. Defensoría del Pueblo — Bolivia's human rights ombudsman, responsible for investigating complaints about government actions affecting fundamental rights, including indigenous and environnemental rights. Bolivia's 2009 Constitution (Article 30) — guarantees indigenous peoples' right to prior consultation on resource extraction in their territories.

YLB (Yacimientos de Litio Bolivianos) — Bolivia's state lithium company, managing lithium industrialization in the Salar de Uyuni. COMIBOL (Corporación Minera de Bolivia) — the state exploitation minière corporation overseeing Bolivia's exploitation minière sector. YPFB (Yacimientos Petrolíferos Fiscales Bolivianos) — the state hydrocarbons company.

Ministerio de Minería y Metalurgia — Bolivia's exploitation minière ministry, responsible for exploitation minière policy and regulation. MMAyA (Ministerio de Medio Ambiente y Agua) — the environment and water ministry, overseeing environnemental policy and water resource management. SENARECOM (Servicio Nacional de Registro y Control de la Comercialización de Minerales y Metales) — the national service for mineral commerce registration and control.

Asamblea Legislativa Plurinacional (Plurinational Legislative Assembly) — Bolivia's bicameral parliament consisting of the Cámara de Diputados (130 members) and the Cámara de Senadores (36 members). Bolivia's 2009 Constitution establishes a 'plurinational' state recognizing indigenous rights and communitarian démocratie. ILO Convention 169 — Bolivia has ratified ILO Convention 169 on Indigenous and Tribal Peoples, requiring free, prior, and informed consultation on resource extraction in indigenous territories.

BS.s: Small island économie ($15.8B PIB) dominated by tourism and financial services (77.2% services). Extreme climate vulnerability — low-lying islands face existential risk from sea level rise and intensifying hurricanes (Hurricane Dorian, 2019, caused $3.4B damage — 25% of PIB).

sectors.w: The Bahamas' offshore financial center enables capital flows that may avoid tax obligations elsewhere. Lorsque beneficial ownership information and tax transparence data are published, the system welfare impact of offshore finance becomes computable.
bp: Mettre en œuvre full beneficial ownership transparence in line with FATF recommendations. Lorsque the competitive advantage of Bahamian financial secrecy is eliminated through transparence, the intérêt chevauchant between offshore service providers and tax-avoiding clients s'affaiblit.

Publish aggregate data on capital flows, tax rulings, and beneficial ownership through enhanced OECD Common Reporting Standard participation. Making tax revenue impacts visible to source jurisdictions makes W computable.

Partiellement cassable. Financial intermediation can relocate — but the Bahamas' geographic proximity to the US and established legal infrastructure create switching costs that give regulation leverage.
a: Report suspicious transactions to the Financial Intelligence Unit (FIU) of The Bahamas
Document beneficial ownership concealment — report to the Securities Commission of The Bahamas
Report regulatory violations to the Central Bank of The Bahamas
Adopter enhanced due diligence procedures for all client relationships
Mettre en œuvre beneficial ownership transparence standards ahead of regulatory mandates
Integrate climate risk assessment into financial products and client advisory services
Central Bank: Renforcer beneficial ownership disclosure requirements for all licensed financial institutions
Securities Commission: Appliquer FATF recommendations on transparence and anti-money laundering
Compliance Commission: Increase supervision of designated non-financial businesses and professions
Contactez votre Member of Parliament in the House of Assembly pour exiger enhanced financial transparence legislation
Support OECD Base Erosion and Profit Shifting (BEPS) Inclusive Framework participation
Leverage the Alliance of Small Island States (AOSIS) to link climate finance to financial transparence commitments
Advocate for Caribbean regional coordination on beneficial ownership transparence
i: FIU (Financial Intelligence Unit of The Bahamas) — the agency responsible for receiving, analyzing, and disseminating suspicious transaction reports. The FIU works with law enforcement to combat money laundering and terrorist financing. Securities Commission of The Bahamas — regulates the investment funds industry and securities market. Central Bank of The Bahamas — the prudential regulator of banks and trust companies.

The Bahamas is home to approximately 200 licensed banks and trust companies. Major international banks maintain operations in Nassau. The Bahamas Services Financiers Board (BFSB) promotes the financial services sector and coordinates industry standards.

Central Bank of The Bahamas — the primary financial regulator and supervisor. The Compliance Commission — supervises non-bank financial service providers for anti-money laundering compliance. FATF (Financial Action Task Force) — The Bahamas has been subject to FATF enhanced monitoring and has implemented reforms to address identified deficiencies in its AML/CFT framework.

Parliament of The Bahamas — bicameral legislature consisting of the House of Assembly (39 members) and the Senate (16 appointed members). Citizens can contact their MP through the official government portal. The Bahamas is a Westminster-style parlementaire démocratie. AOSIS (Alliance of Small Island States) — The Bahamas is an active member, advocating for climate action and sea level rise mitigation. CARICOM — provides regional coordination on financial regulation.

BW.s: Upper-middle-income économie ($19.4B PIB) with diamond exploitation minière historically driving growth (Debswana JV with De Beers). Services-dominant (63.5% PIB). One of Africa's most stable democracies. Diversification challenge as lab-grown diamonds threaten natural diamond demand.

sectors.w: Lab-grown diamonds are disrupting the natural diamond market (30%+ of gem market by volume). As synthetic alternatives become indistinguishable and cheaper, the intérêt chevauchant between Debswana and consumers in natural diamond premium s'affaiblit — Botswana must diversify.
bp: Accelerate economic diversification using Pula Fund sovereign wealth. Lorsque non-diamond revenue streams (tech, tourism, beef) exceed declining diamond revenues, l'intérêt chevauchant dans continued diamond dependence breaks naturally.

Publish full lifecycle environnemental data (water use, land disturbance, energy consumption) per carat for all exploitation minière operations. Making environnemental costs visible makes W computable from diamond production data.

Partiellement cassable. Open-pit diamond exploitation minière inherently disturbs landscapes. However, Botswana's mines are in relatively low-biodiversity Kalahari terrain, and rehabilitation programs can reduce long-term dépendance du système.
a: Report environnemental violations at mine sites to DEA (Department of Environmental Affairs)
Document community displacement or inadequate compensation — report to the Ombudsman
Report safety violations to the Department of Mines
Debswana: Développer post-diamond economic transition plan with énergie renouvelable investment
De Beers: Extend lifecycle environnemental reporting to all Botswana operations
Adopter Kimberley Process Certification Scheme enhanced standards including environnemental metrics
Ministry of Minerals and Energy: Require environnemental bonds for all exploitation minière licenses
BURS (Botswana Unified Revenue Service): Ensure mineral royalties reflect true environnemental costs
Okavango Diamond Company: Lead on sustainability certification for Botswana diamonds
Contactez votre Member of Parliament in the National Assembly pour exiger a comprehensive post-diamond economic diversification strategy
Support African Union Mining Vision implementation for sustainable diamond governance
Advocate for Kimberley Process reform to include environnemental and social standards
Pousser pour SADC (Southern African Développerment Community) coordination on exploitation minière sustainability
i: DEA (Department of Environmental Affairs) — under the Ministry of Environment and Tourism, responsible for environnemental policy, impact assessment, and monitoring. Department of Mines — under the Ministry of Minerals and Energy, responsible for exploitation minière safety regulation, licensing, and compliance monitoring. Office of the Ombudsman — investigates complaints against government agencies and public officials.

Debswana Diamond Company — a 50/50 joint venture between the Government of Botswana and De Beers Group, operating the Jwaneng and Orapa mines. Debswana is Botswana's largest private employer and the largest contributor to government revenue. De Beers Group (Anglo American subsidiary) — the global diamond company partnering with Botswana. The 2023 renegotiation of the Debswana agreement gave Botswana a greater share of rough diamond sales.

Ministry of Minerals and Energy — oversees exploitation minière policy and the regulatory framework under the Mines and Minerals Act. BURS (Botswana Unified Revenue Service) — the tax authority responsible for collecting mineral royalties and taxes. Pula Fund — Botswana's sovereign wealth fund (managed by the Bank of Botswana), designed to save diamond revenues for future generations.

National Assembly of Botswana — unicameral parliament with 65 members (57 elected, 8 specially elected). Citizens can contact their MPs through the parlementaire website. Botswana is one of Africa's most stable multiparty democracies, with peaceful transfers of power since independence in 1966. The Ntlo ya Dikgosi (House of Chiefs) — an advisory body representing traditional leaders, with input on matters affecting tribal territories including exploitation minière.

BY.s: State-controlled économie ($76B PIB) with significant manufacturing (20.3% PIB). Potash exploitation minière (Belaruskali is one of the world's largest producers). Oil refining (Naftan, Mozyr) processes Russian crude. Authoritarian governance under Lukashenko since 1994.

sectors.w: Belarus is the world's third-largest potash producer (Belaruskali). Potash exploitation minière generates massive salt waste (solukhards) that contaminate groundwater and rivers. Lorsque downstream water contamination data is published alongside potash production revenue, W devient calculable.
bp: International sanctions (EU, US, UK since 2021) have already weakened the intérêt chevauchant by restricting Belaruskali's market access. Lorsque sanctioned export revenues fall below environnemental remediation costs, the economic case for continued expansion breaks.

Satellite-based environnemental monitoring (salt waste accumulation, water contamination plumes) published by international organizations. Making environnemental damage visible from space bypasses domestic data suppression and makes W computable.

Partiellement cassable. Potash exploitation minière inherently generates salt waste. However, solution exploitation minière and backfill techniques can reduce surface waste accumulation and groundwater contamination.
a: Document environnemental violations through secure channels to international environnemental organizations
Report safety concerns through ILO complaint mechanisms
Provide data to international monitoring organizations (Belarusian diaspora networks)
International potash buyers: Demand environnemental certification as condition of purchase (post-sanctions)
Apply IFC Performance Standards to any future investment in Belarusian exploitation minière
Require independent environnemental audits as part of off-take agreements
International sanctions bodies: Maintain and strengthen potash export sanctions until environnemental and governance reforms are implemented
UNECE: Continue environnemental performance reviews of Belarus under the Convention on Environmental Impact Assessment (Espoo Convention)
IndustriALL: Monitor labor rights conditions at Belaruskali
Support maintenance of international sanctions on Belaruskali until environnemental and governance reforms are implemented
Plaider via UNECE for transboundary environnemental monitoring of potash exploitation minière impacts
Support Belarusian democratic opposition's platform for environnemental and governance reform
Pousser pour international environnemental damage assessment of Soligorsk exploitation minière district
i: Note: Belarus under Lukashenko is rated 'Not Free' by Freedom House. Independent media, société civile, and trade unions face severe repression. There is no effective lanceur d'alerte protection. After the 2020 protests, thousands were imprisoned and major independent organizations were forcibly liquidated. Safe reporting channels include the Belarusian diaspora network, international trade union federations (IndustriALL Global Union), and international environnemental organizations.

Belaruskali — Belarus's d'État potash company, one of the world's largest potash producers. Belaruskali operates six mines near Soligorsk. The company has been under EU, US, and UK sanctions since 2021 following the forced diversion of Ryanair Flight 4978 and repression of democratic opposition. BPC (Belarusian Potash Company) — the former exclusive export trading arm of Belaruskali (sanctioned). Competitors include Nutrien (Canada), Mosaic (US), and K+S (Germany).

Note: Domestic regulatory institutions (Ministry of Natural Resources and Environmental Protection) are not independent under autoritaire governance. International mechanisms include: UNECE (United Nations Economic Commission for Europe) — Belarus is party to the Espoo Convention and the Convention on the Protection and Use of Transboundary Watercourses. EU sanctions framework — Council Regulation (EU) restricting trade with Belaruskali and associated entities.

Note: Belarus has no functioning democratic legislature. The National Assembly (Natsionalnoye Sobranie) is not freely elected. International engagement channels: EU Eastern Partnership framework, OSCE (Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe), United Nations Human Rights Council Universal Periodic Review. The Belarusian democratic opposition in exile (led by Sviatlana Tsikhanouskaya) maintains a platform including environnemental governance reform.

CD.s: Resource-rich but impoverished économie ($71B PIB). Mining dominates (28.81% of PIB) — world's largest cobalt producer (70%+ global supply), significant copper, coltan, and tin. Conflict minerals remain a governance challenge. Agricultural employment is 60%+ but only 9.6% of PIB.

sectors.w: DRC's cobalt is essential for EV batteries — downstream manufacturers (Tesla, Samsung SDI, CATL) face increasing ESG pressure. Lorsque supply chain traceability makes mine-level environnemental and labor conditions visible to end consumers, W devient calculable from cobalt prices.
